--- title: 'Unit 3 Report: Nuclear Reactor Cooling Timeline' format: html highlight-style: default code-block-bg: '#000000' filters: - shinylive --- # Unit 3 Modeling Report ## Corey Silver and Parker Lee ### Scenario: Cooling Performance During Reactor Shutdown Engineers are studying how quickly reactor components cool following shutdown. # 1. Measurements: | Time after Shutdown ($\text{hr}$) | Temperature $(^\circ C)$| |---------|:-----| | 1 | 580 | | 2 | 510 | | 4 | 430 | | 8 | 340 | |12 | 290 | |18 | 230 | ::: {#f7632e68 .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import numpy as np from htmltools.svg import title import scipy as sci import pandas as pd import IPython.display as ipd from shinylive import * from data_fitting import * X = [1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 18] # See section 3 for explanation of modification of T data. Y = [1.05*y for y in [580, 510, 430, 340, 290, 230]] ``` ::: "A large prediction error could affect cooling procedures and operational decisions. Your team has been asked to recommend the best model and fitting strategy." # 2. Objective Definition We need to reliably predict the temperature of the reactor (in degrees Centigrade) from the time passed since shutoff (in hours). Since this is a live nuclear reactor, criticality is a prime safety concern. # 3. Assumptions and Simplifications In any mathematical model, there are certain assumptions made and simplifications accepted. For this model, we keep in mind the sensitive nature of the system in question, which leades us to an overarching principle which we'll follow throughout this modelling process. ## Our first and central assumption is that of the *worst-case scenario*: we assume the least optimal conditions, in every case, for time to cooling. For example, when we assume a constant external temperature, we assume that temperature to be the mean daily maximum for the season in which we operate. We assume high humidity that will slow the cooling rate for the reactor components, etc. 1. Bulk, uniform cooling rate: no regions of greater $T$ density 1. Single substance component: single specific heat 1. Constant external temperature 1. Constant external humidity, pressure: We assume no weather. 1. We assume the absence of other heat sources, ignoring the effect of sunlight. 1. Reactor isn't operated until cooling is complete 1. We assume a $-5{\%}$ margin of error with temperature measurement (The observed temeperatures have been increased by a factor of $1.05$). This will be one in a group of safety redundancies. # 4. Variables and Parameters $T_R$ is the reactor temperature. $T_E$ is the external temperature $t$ is the time from shutdown in hours. That's it! While a high-fidelity model would and could account for the specific heats of individual reactor components, the role of humidity in cooling, and the stochastic nature of thermodynamic processes, we can simplify extensively for the purpose of this problem. Furthermore, we're not supplied with external temperature data. Therefore, we'll leave out the external temperature as an explicit variable and **we'll model the cooling rate exclusively in terms of the reactor temperature and time passed.** First, we're supplied with cooling and time data; this means that we must assume similar external conditions throughout the cooling period in order to use this supplied data for interpolation and limited extrapolation. Second, we can justify some simplification from the safety-focused nature of the model we're designing. We do not know the external conditions for the data we're given, so the most prudent approach is to assume that these conditions are favorable to quick cooling and consider them optimized. We will start, at least, from a basic model of heat transfer. This is most appropriate for the limited data we have on the situation. # 5. Model Development We'll test optimized Chebyshev, Least-Squares, and Absolute-Deviation fits of the following models: ## Linear: Of form $T(t)=c_1\cdot t + c_2$. ::: {#e89299d9 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} poly_deg = 1 plotter(X, Y, "linear", 1) coeff_table(X, Y, "linear", 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
Function Parameters
c0 c1
Optimization Type Least Squares -20.415313 569.614849
Absolute Deviation -21.617647 580.279412
Chebyshev -23.099990 581.699903
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](U03_REPORT_files/figure-html/cell-3-output-2.png){width=657 height=1905} ::: ::: ## Polynomial: Of form $T(t)=c_1t^n + c_2t^{n-1} +...+c_kt^0$. We'll limit $n$ and $k$ to four or five, at most. Any higher and at that point we'd be overfitting to approximate a power curve. Overfitting can introduce enormous spikes in a polynomial fit due to the Gibbs Phenomenon (I think). Please use the slider below to observe the effect of polynomial degree on fit parameters and optimization quality. ```{shinylive-python} #| standalone: true #| viewerHeight: 500 """ code modified from example in docs at https://quarto-ext.github.io/shinylive/ This gemini conversation used for debugging and editing: https://gemini.google.com/share/59ac2467bf7b """ from data_fitting import * from shiny import * import shinywidgets as shw app_ui = ui.page_fluid( ui.layout_columns( ui.div( ui.card( ui.h4("Poly-fitter"), ui.input_slider("n", "Degree of Polynomial Fit:", min=1, max=20, value=2), bg="#C0C0C0" ), ui.card( ui.card_header("Fit Parameters"), ui.output_table("coefficients"), style="overflow-x: auto;" # Keeps wide tables behaving cleanly ) ), # Visual Card for the Plot ui.card( ui.card_header("Optimization-type Comparison"), ui.output_plot("conspiracy"), full_screen=True # Adds a neat zoom button for the user ), # Visual Card for the Data Table col_widths=[7, 5], # Allocates 7/12ths of width to plot, 5/12ths to table title = "Degree Comparison for Polynomial Fits") ) def server(input: Inputs, output: Outputs, session: Session): @render.plot def conspiracy(): poly_deg = input.n() return plotter(X, Y, "polynomial", poly_deg) @render.table def coefficients(): poly_deg = input.n() return coeff_table(X, Y, "polynomial", poly_deg) app = App(app_ui, server) ## file: data_fitting.py #!/usr/bin/env python # coding: utf-8 # In[6]: """ data_fitting.py This file contains functions for data fitting in MATH 283 author: Corey Silver date: 3/6/26 (Yuro Style) functions require numpy, scipy, matplotlib, sympy libraries debugging suggestions (frequently wrong) from gemini: https://gemini.google.com/share/de2be7b1f7ee """ import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import numpy as np import scipy as sci import pandas as pd import IPython.display as ipd # import shiny as sh # import shinywidgets as shw func_types = ["linear", "polynomial", "power", "exponential"] # poly_deg = 2 """ poly_deg = int( input("What degree of polynomial? Please give your answer as a numeral.") ) """ degree_list = ["zeroth", "linear", "quadratic", "cubic", "quartic", "quintic"] k = 0 while k < 95: degree_list.append(f"{k + 6}th degree") k += 1 def main(X, Y, n=2): """ The big Huncho Grande Paparoni: fitting, error analysis, plotting. """ # func_types = ["linear", "polynomial", "power", "exponential"] table_list = [] for func in func_types: f = functionator(X, Y, func, n) X, Y, k, func_type = f[0:5] # plotter(X, Y, n, func_type) entry = error_analyzer(X, Y, k) table_list.append(entry) table_du_fromage = pd.DataFrame( table_list, index=pd.MultiIndex.from_product( [["Fit Type"], ["linear", degree_list[n], "power", "exponential"]] ), columns=pd.MultiIndex.from_product( [ ["Optimization Type"], ["Least Squares", "Absolute Deviation", "Chebyshev"], ] ), ) # ipd.display(table_du_fromage) # ipd.Markdown(table_du_fromage.to_markdown(index=False)) return table_du_fromage def functionator(X, Y, func_type, n): """ Takes X, Y, and func type. Modifies X and Y if needed returns X, Y, n, func_type as list """ fit_type = func_type k = n if fit_type == "polynomial": fit_type = degree_list[n] elif fit_type == "exponential": Y = np.log(Y) k = 1 elif fit_type == "power": Y = np.log(Y) X = np.log(X) k = 1 elif fit_type == "linear": k = 1 else: print("Request cannot be completed, defaulting to rat") import matplotlib.image as mpimg img = mpimg.imread("fat_rats/Joanna_Servaes_wikimedia_commons.webp") plt.imshow(img) plt.axis("off") plt.imshow(img) plt.axis("off") plt.imshow(img) plt.axis("off") plt.show() fit_type = None return return [X, Y, k, fit_type] def error_analyzer(X, Y, n, k=-1): """ prints results of least squares, chebyshev, and absolute deviation analyses for type of model given by model_name and degree given by n. For error up to an arbitrary point, edit indices of the individual error functions. Default value of k is -1. """ LS2 = leastsquerror(Y, np.polyval(LS2_fit(X, Y, n), X))[k] AbsDev = absdev_error(Y, np.polyval(absdev_fit(X, Y, n), X))[k] Cheby = cheb_error(Y, np.polyval(chebyshevify(X, Y, n), X)) """ print(f"For the {model_name} model") print( f"We found a least squares error of {LS2}" ) print( f"We found an absolute deviation error of {AbsDev}" ) print( f"We found a Chebyshev error of {Cheby}" ) """ return [LS2, AbsDev, Cheby] def leastsquerror(Y, F): """ Takes two lists, Y = [data points] and F(x) = [func_values]. Computes cumulative variances of a series of residuals returns list of variances """ resid = residuals(Y, F) squares = [] squm = [] for r in resid: squares.append(r**2) squm.append(sum(squares)) return squm def absdev_error(Y, F): """ Takes two lists, Y = [data points] and F(x) = [func_values]. computes cumulative absolute deviation returns list of absolute deviations """ resid = residuals(Y, F) normals = [] absum = [] for r in resid: normals.append(abs(r)) absum.append(sum(normals)) return absum def cheb_error(Y, F): """ Takes two lists, Y = [data points] and F(x) = [func_values]. returns maximum residual """ cheb = abs(max(residuals(Y, F))) return cheb def residuals(Y, F, func_type="linear"): """ Takes two lists, Y = [data points] and F(x) = [func_values] computes their residuals returns list of residuals """ resid = [] if func_type == "exponential" or func_type == "power": Y = [np.e**y for y in Y] else: pass for f, y in zip(F, Y): resid.append(y - f) return resid def arrayer(x, Y): """ requires numpy takes two lists and makes them into arrays """ x_array = np.array(x) Y_array = np.array(Y) return x_array, Y_array def objectivist(X, Y, n): """ Takes two arrays, x = [data 1] and Y = [data 2]. f(x) = c_n*x^n + c_(n-1)x^(n-1)+...c_0x^0 with n parameters returns objective function f """ mat_x = [] mat_y = [] x_list = [] for Xi, Yi in zip(X, Y): i = n rightrow = [] leftrow = [] objective = [] bounds = [] while i > -1: bounds.append((None, None)) objective.append(0) rightrow.append(Xi**i) leftrow.append(-(Xi**i)) x_list.append(Xi**i) i -= 1 rightrow.append(-1) leftrow.append(-1) mat_x.append(rightrow) mat_x.append(leftrow) mat_y.append(Yi) mat_y.append(-Yi) bounds.append((0, None)) objective.append(1) barry = [objective, mat_x, mat_y, bounds, x_list] return barry def chebyshevify(X, Y, n): """ Takes two arrays, x = [data 1] and Y = [data 2]. Using Chebyshev criteria, fits model function f(x) = c_n*x^n + c_(n-1)x^(n-1)+...c_0x^0 with n parameters such that (max|Yi-Fi|, i ϵ NN) is minimized. returns F(x) as an array of coefficients, the last being the max error E ########################################################################################################### ## https://byui.instructure.com/courses/409534/pages/w07-tuesday-lesson-plans-2?module_item_id=4500264 ## ########################################################################################################### used as guideline and partial template """ """ mat_x = [] mat_y = [] objective = [] x = sym.symbol("x") for xi, Yi in zip(X, Y): rightrow = [] leftrow = [] bounds = [] x_list = [] while n > -1: rightrow.append(xi**n) leftrow.append(-(xi**n)) x_list.append(x**n) bounds.append((None, None)) objective.append(0) n -= 1 rightrow.append(-1) leftrow.append(-1) mat_x.append(rightrow) mat_x.append(leftrow) mat_y.append(Yi) mat_y.append(-Yi) bounds[-1] = (0, None) objective.append(1) """ barry = objectivist(X, Y, n) result = sci.optimize.linprog( barry[0], A_ub=barry[1], b_ub=barry[2], bounds=barry[3], method="highs" ) result = np.delete(result.x, -1) return result def sum_maker(X, Y, n): """ Takes two arrays, x = [data 1] and Y = [data 2] and desired polynomial degree n. Returns a matrix of sums of x values and a matrix of sums of y values """ i = n sum_list_you_got_there = [] neg_sum_list = [] while i > -1: power_list = [] neg_pow_list = [] for Xi in X: it = Xi**i power_list.append(it) neg_pow_list.append(-it) sum_list_you_got_there.append(sum(power_list)) neg_sum_list.append(sum(neg_pow_list)) i -= 1 x_mat = [sum_list_you_got_there, neg_sum_list] y_mat = [sum(Y), -sum(Y)] return [x_mat, y_mat] def absdev_fit(X, Y, n): """ takes X data and makes a list of powers (X^n, X^n-1...), dots it with undefined params to whip up a cost function that absdev_fit will minimize ##################################################################################################################### ## https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51883058/l1-norm-instead-of-l2-norm-for-cost-function-in-regression-model ## ##################################################################################################################### used as guideline and partial template """ def cost_func(params): # sympy removal courtesy of gemini thread listed up top # SciPy will plug the current numerical guess into 'params' f = np.polyval(params, X) # Return the L1 norm (sum of absolute deviations) return np.sum(np.abs(Y - f)) """ f = np.polyval(params, X) cost_func = np.sum(np.abs(Y - f)) """ guess = (n + 1) * [1.0] result = sci.optimize.minimize(cost_func, guess, method="Nelder-Mead") return result.x def LS2_fit(X, Y, n): """ Takes two arrays, x = [data 1] and Y = [data 2]. fits model function f(x) = c_n*x^n + c_(n-1)x^(n-1)+...c_0x^0 with n parameters such that (sum|Yi-Fi|^2, i ϵ NN) is minimized. returns f(x) as an array """ LS2 = np.polyfit(X, Y, n) return LS2 def x_list(X): """ takes X data, returns linspaced list of X-powers from X^n to X^0. """ X = np.linspace(X[0], X[-1] + np.mean(X) / 1000, 1000) return X def fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n): """ Takes X, Y, and polynomial degree n. Grabs coefficients from the fit generators and returns arrays of actual function output. """ LS2_coeffs = LS2_fit(X, Y, n) cheb_coeffs = chebyshevify(X, Y, n) abs_dev_coeffs = absdev_fit(X, Y, n) exes = x_list(X) # print(f"Least squares fitting coeffs: {LS2_coeffs}") # print(f"Chebyshev fit coeffs: {cheb_coeffs}") # print(f"Absolute deviation fit coeffs: {abs_dev_coeffs}") LS2 = np.polyval(LS2_coeffs, exes) cheb = np.polyval(cheb_coeffs, exes) absdev = np.polyval(abs_dev_coeffs, exes) return [exes, LS2, cheb, absdev] def coeff_table(X, Y, func_type, n): """ Takes X, Y, polynomial degree n. Grabs fits and returns their parameters for different func_types in a table format. """ # for func in func_types: table_list = [] f = functionator(X, Y, func_type, n) X, Y, n, func_type = f[0:5] LS2_coeffs = LS2_fit(X, Y, n) cheb_coeffs = chebyshevify(X, Y, n) abs_dev_coeffs = absdev_fit(X, Y, n) coeffs_list = [LS2_coeffs, cheb_coeffs, abs_dev_coeffs] c_list = [f"c{c}" for c in range(n + 1)] for coeff in coeffs_list: entry = coeff table_list.append(entry) table_du_fromage = pd.DataFrame( table_list, index=pd.MultiIndex.from_product( [ ["Optimization Type"], ["Least Squares", "Absolute Deviation", "Chebyshev"], ] ), columns=pd.MultiIndex.from_product([["Function Parameters"], c_list]), ) # ipd.display(table_du_fromage, display_id=func_type) # ipd.Markdown(table_du_fromage.to_markdown(index=False)) return table_du_fromage def plotter(X, Y, func_type, n, font_size=12): """ Plots different fit functions for a given data fit type (linear, poly, etc) """ X, Y, n, func_type = functionator(X, Y, func_type, n)[0:4] exes = fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n)[0] LS2 = fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n)[1] cheb = fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n)[2] absdev = fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n)[3] # Labels so I don't have to type them thrice xlab = "Hours passed since Reactor Shutdown" ylab = "Reactor temperature in $^{\\circ} C$" fig1 = plt.figure(figsize=(7, 20), layout="tight") # fig1.suptitle(f"Below are the optimizations for a {func_type} fit") plt.rcParams["font.size"] = font_size # plt.rcParams["axes.labelpad"] = 10 dist = 40 pointsize = 40 point_color = "black" ax1 = plt.subplot(3, 1, 1) ax1.scatter(X, Y, s=pointsize, c=point_color) ax1.plot(exes, LS2, color="r") ax1.set_xlabel(xlab) ax1.set_ylabel(ylab, rotation=55, labelpad=(dist), fontsize=12) ax1.set_title("Least-Squares Fit") ax2 = plt.subplot(3, 1, 2) ax2.scatter(X, Y, s=pointsize, c=point_color) ax2.plot(exes, cheb, color="y") ax2.set_xlabel(xlab) ax2.set_ylabel(ylab, rotation=55, labelpad=(dist), fontsize=12) ax2.set_title("Chebyshev Fit") ax3 = plt.subplot(3, 1, 3) plt.scatter(X, Y, s=pointsize, c=point_color) plt.plot(exes, absdev, color="b") ax3.set_xlabel(xlab) ax3.set_ylabel(ylab, rotation=55, labelpad=(dist), fontsize=12) ax3.set_title("Absolute Deviation Fit") def temp_finder(X, Y, n): """ Takes X, Y, n then obtains user input for time since reactor shutdown (Will add this feature later). Returns projected temp as calculated from Chebyshev-optimized exponential fit """ """ x = float(input("How many hours has it been since the reactor shut down")) """ x = 24 X, Y, n = functionator(X, Y, "exponential", n)[0:3] # LS2_coeffs = LS2_fit(X, Y, n) cheb_coeffs = chebyshevify(X, Y, n) # abs_dev_coeffs = absdev_fit(X, Y, n) # LS2 = np.polyval(LS2_coeffs, x) cheb = np.polyval(cheb_coeffs, x) # absdev = np.polyval(abs_dev_coeffs, x) T = f"After {x} hours, we project an estimated reactor temperature of {cheb:.3f} degrees centigrade." return T X = [1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 18] Y = [580, 510, 430, 340, 290, 230] if __name__ == "__main__": main(X, Y) coeff_table(X, Y, "linear", 1) # temp_finder(X, Y) # plotter(X, Y, "polynomial") # %% ## file: requirements.txt matplotlib numpy scipy pandas IPython ``` ## Exponential decay: Of form $T(t) = (c_2)e^{c_1t}$ ($c_2$ is negative). We'll linearize this for two reasons; so that this fit can be optimized with linear programming and for the sake of visual clarity. Taking the natural log of both sides gives us $$ \ln[T(t)]=c_1t+c_2. $$ Simple inspection of the plots makes it look as though the exponential model fits the data most closely. We cannot confirm this hunch, however, until we do some analysis on the error of the various plots. ::: {#80eaa803 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} poly_deg = 1 plotter(X, Y, "exponential", 1) coeff_table(X, Y, "exponential", 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
Function Parameters
c0 c1
Optimization Type Least Squares -0.052638 6.376594
Absolute Deviation -0.054409 6.389616
Chebyshev -0.056442 6.395988
``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](U03_REPORT_files/figure-html/cell-4-output-2.png){width=657 height=1905} ::: ::: ## Power model: Of form $T(t) = (c_2)^{c_1t}$ We'll linearize this sort of model in the same way, also giving us what looks like a strong candidate for a predictive and interpolative fit. $$ \ln[T(t)]=c_1t+c_2. $$ ::: {#7b57b9f4 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} poly_deg = 1 plotter(X, Y, "power", 1) coeff_table(X, Y, "power", 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ```{=html}

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](U03_REPORT_files/figure-html/cell-5-output-2.png){width=657 height=1905} ::: ::: # 6. Interpretation Results of error analysis are given in the table below. ```{shinylive-python} #| standalone: true #| viewerHeight: 800 """ code modified from example in docs at https://quarto-ext.github.io/shinylive/ This gemini conversation used for debugging and editing: https://gemini.google.com/share/59ac2467bf7b """ from data_fitting import * from shiny import * import shinywidgets as shw app_ui = ui.page_fluid( ui.layout_columns( ui.div( ui.input_slider("n", "Degree of Polynomial Fit:", min=1, max=20, value = 2), ui.output_table("error_table") ) ) ) def server(input: Inputs, output: Outputs, session: Session): """ @render.data_frame def error_table(): poly_deg = input.n() return main(X, Y, poly_deg) """ @render.table def error_table(): poly_deg = input.n() return main(X, Y, n=poly_deg) app = App(app_ui, server) ## file: data_fitting.py #!/usr/bin/env python # coding: utf-8 # In[6]: """ data_fitting.py This file contains functions for data fitting in MATH 283 author: Corey Silver date: 3/6/26 (Yuro Style) functions require numpy, scipy, matplotlib, sympy libraries debugging suggestions (frequently wrong) from gemini: https://gemini.google.com/share/de2be7b1f7ee """ import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import numpy as np import scipy as sci import pandas as pd import IPython.display as ipd # import shiny as sh # import shinywidgets as shw func_types = ["linear", "polynomial", "power", "exponential"] # poly_deg = 2 """ poly_deg = int( input("What degree of polynomial? Please give your answer as a numeral.") ) """ degree_list = ["zeroth", "linear", "quadratic", "cubic", "quartic", "quintic"] k = 0 while k < 95: degree_list.append(f"{k + 6}th degree") k += 1 def main(X, Y, n=2): """ The big Huncho Grande Paparoni: fitting, error analysis, plotting. """ # func_types = ["linear", "polynomial", "power", "exponential"] table_list = [] for func in func_types: f = functionator(X, Y, func, n) X, Y, k, func_type = f[0:5] # plotter(X, Y, n, func_type) entry = error_analyzer(X, Y, k) table_list.append(entry) table_du_fromage = pd.DataFrame( table_list, index=pd.MultiIndex.from_product( [["Fit Type"], ["linear", degree_list[n], "power", "exponential"]] ), columns=pd.MultiIndex.from_product( [ ["Optimization Type"], ["Least Squares", "Absolute Deviation", "Chebyshev"], ] ), ) # ipd.display(table_du_fromage) # ipd.Markdown(table_du_fromage.to_markdown(index=False)) return table_du_fromage def functionator(X, Y, func_type, n): """ Takes X, Y, and func type. Modifies X and Y if needed returns X, Y, n, func_type as list """ fit_type = func_type k = n if fit_type == "polynomial": fit_type = degree_list[n] elif fit_type == "exponential": Y = np.log(Y) k = 1 elif fit_type == "power": Y = np.log(Y) X = np.log(X) k = 1 elif fit_type == "linear": k = 1 else: print("Request cannot be completed, defaulting to rat") import matplotlib.image as mpimg img = mpimg.imread("fat_rats/Joanna_Servaes_wikimedia_commons.webp") plt.imshow(img) plt.axis("off") plt.imshow(img) plt.axis("off") plt.imshow(img) plt.axis("off") plt.show() fit_type = None return return [X, Y, k, fit_type] def error_analyzer(X, Y, n, k=-1): """ prints results of least squares, chebyshev, and absolute deviation analyses for type of model given by model_name and degree given by n. For error up to an arbitrary point, edit indices of the individual error functions. Default value of k is -1. """ LS2 = leastsquerror(Y, np.polyval(LS2_fit(X, Y, n), X))[k] AbsDev = absdev_error(Y, np.polyval(absdev_fit(X, Y, n), X))[k] Cheby = cheb_error(Y, np.polyval(chebyshevify(X, Y, n), X)) """ print(f"For the {model_name} model") print( f"We found a least squares error of {LS2}" ) print( f"We found an absolute deviation error of {AbsDev}" ) print( f"We found a Chebyshev error of {Cheby}" ) """ return [LS2, AbsDev, Cheby] def leastsquerror(Y, F): """ Takes two lists, Y = [data points] and F(x) = [func_values]. Computes cumulative variances of a series of residuals returns list of variances """ resid = residuals(Y, F) squares = [] squm = [] for r in resid: squares.append(r**2) squm.append(sum(squares)) return squm def absdev_error(Y, F): """ Takes two lists, Y = [data points] and F(x) = [func_values]. computes cumulative absolute deviation returns list of absolute deviations """ resid = residuals(Y, F) normals = [] absum = [] for r in resid: normals.append(abs(r)) absum.append(sum(normals)) return absum def cheb_error(Y, F): """ Takes two lists, Y = [data points] and F(x) = [func_values]. returns maximum residual """ cheb = abs(max(residuals(Y, F))) return cheb def residuals(Y, F, func_type="linear"): """ Takes two lists, Y = [data points] and F(x) = [func_values] computes their residuals returns list of residuals """ resid = [] if func_type == "exponential" or func_type == "power": Y = [np.e**y for y in Y] else: pass for f, y in zip(F, Y): resid.append(y - f) return resid def arrayer(x, Y): """ requires numpy takes two lists and makes them into arrays """ x_array = np.array(x) Y_array = np.array(Y) return x_array, Y_array def objectivist(X, Y, n): """ Takes two arrays, x = [data 1] and Y = [data 2]. f(x) = c_n*x^n + c_(n-1)x^(n-1)+...c_0x^0 with n parameters returns objective function f """ mat_x = [] mat_y = [] x_list = [] for Xi, Yi in zip(X, Y): i = n rightrow = [] leftrow = [] objective = [] bounds = [] while i > -1: bounds.append((None, None)) objective.append(0) rightrow.append(Xi**i) leftrow.append(-(Xi**i)) x_list.append(Xi**i) i -= 1 rightrow.append(-1) leftrow.append(-1) mat_x.append(rightrow) mat_x.append(leftrow) mat_y.append(Yi) mat_y.append(-Yi) bounds.append((0, None)) objective.append(1) barry = [objective, mat_x, mat_y, bounds, x_list] return barry def chebyshevify(X, Y, n): """ Takes two arrays, x = [data 1] and Y = [data 2]. Using Chebyshev criteria, fits model function f(x) = c_n*x^n + c_(n-1)x^(n-1)+...c_0x^0 with n parameters such that (max|Yi-Fi|, i ϵ NN) is minimized. returns F(x) as an array of coefficients, the last being the max error E ########################################################################################################### ## https://byui.instructure.com/courses/409534/pages/w07-tuesday-lesson-plans-2?module_item_id=4500264 ## ########################################################################################################### used as guideline and partial template """ """ mat_x = [] mat_y = [] objective = [] x = sym.symbol("x") for xi, Yi in zip(X, Y): rightrow = [] leftrow = [] bounds = [] x_list = [] while n > -1: rightrow.append(xi**n) leftrow.append(-(xi**n)) x_list.append(x**n) bounds.append((None, None)) objective.append(0) n -= 1 rightrow.append(-1) leftrow.append(-1) mat_x.append(rightrow) mat_x.append(leftrow) mat_y.append(Yi) mat_y.append(-Yi) bounds[-1] = (0, None) objective.append(1) """ barry = objectivist(X, Y, n) result = sci.optimize.linprog( barry[0], A_ub=barry[1], b_ub=barry[2], bounds=barry[3], method="highs" ) result = np.delete(result.x, -1) return result def sum_maker(X, Y, n): """ Takes two arrays, x = [data 1] and Y = [data 2] and desired polynomial degree n. Returns a matrix of sums of x values and a matrix of sums of y values """ i = n sum_list_you_got_there = [] neg_sum_list = [] while i > -1: power_list = [] neg_pow_list = [] for Xi in X: it = Xi**i power_list.append(it) neg_pow_list.append(-it) sum_list_you_got_there.append(sum(power_list)) neg_sum_list.append(sum(neg_pow_list)) i -= 1 x_mat = [sum_list_you_got_there, neg_sum_list] y_mat = [sum(Y), -sum(Y)] return [x_mat, y_mat] def absdev_fit(X, Y, n): """ takes X data and makes a list of powers (X^n, X^n-1...), dots it with undefined params to whip up a cost function that absdev_fit will minimize ##################################################################################################################### ## https://stackoverflow.com/questions/51883058/l1-norm-instead-of-l2-norm-for-cost-function-in-regression-model ## ##################################################################################################################### used as guideline and partial template """ def cost_func(params): # sympy removal courtesy of gemini thread listed up top # SciPy will plug the current numerical guess into 'params' f = np.polyval(params, X) # Return the L1 norm (sum of absolute deviations) return np.sum(np.abs(Y - f)) """ f = np.polyval(params, X) cost_func = np.sum(np.abs(Y - f)) """ guess = (n + 1) * [1.0] result = sci.optimize.minimize(cost_func, guess, method="Nelder-Mead") return result.x def LS2_fit(X, Y, n): """ Takes two arrays, x = [data 1] and Y = [data 2]. fits model function f(x) = c_n*x^n + c_(n-1)x^(n-1)+...c_0x^0 with n parameters such that (sum|Yi-Fi|^2, i ϵ NN) is minimized. returns f(x) as an array """ LS2 = np.polyfit(X, Y, n) return LS2 def x_list(X): """ takes X data, returns linspaced list of X-powers from X^n to X^0. """ X = np.linspace(X[0], X[-1] + np.mean(X) / 1000, 1000) return X def fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n): """ Takes X, Y, and polynomial degree n. Grabs coefficients from the fit generators and returns arrays of actual function output. """ LS2_coeffs = LS2_fit(X, Y, n) cheb_coeffs = chebyshevify(X, Y, n) abs_dev_coeffs = absdev_fit(X, Y, n) exes = x_list(X) # print(f"Least squares fitting coeffs: {LS2_coeffs}") # print(f"Chebyshev fit coeffs: {cheb_coeffs}") # print(f"Absolute deviation fit coeffs: {abs_dev_coeffs}") LS2 = np.polyval(LS2_coeffs, exes) cheb = np.polyval(cheb_coeffs, exes) absdev = np.polyval(abs_dev_coeffs, exes) return [exes, LS2, cheb, absdev] def coeff_table(X, Y, func_type, n): """ Takes X, Y, polynomial degree n. Grabs fits and returns their parameters for different func_types in a table format. """ # for func in func_types: table_list = [] f = functionator(X, Y, func_type, n) X, Y, n, func_type = f[0:5] LS2_coeffs = LS2_fit(X, Y, n) cheb_coeffs = chebyshevify(X, Y, n) abs_dev_coeffs = absdev_fit(X, Y, n) coeffs_list = [LS2_coeffs, cheb_coeffs, abs_dev_coeffs] c_list = [f"c{c}" for c in range(n + 1)] for coeff in coeffs_list: entry = coeff table_list.append(entry) table_du_fromage = pd.DataFrame( table_list, index=pd.MultiIndex.from_product( [ ["Optimization Type"], ["Least Squares", "Absolute Deviation", "Chebyshev"], ] ), columns=pd.MultiIndex.from_product([["Function Parameters"], c_list]), ) # ipd.display(table_du_fromage, display_id=func_type) # ipd.Markdown(table_du_fromage.to_markdown(index=False)) return table_du_fromage def plotter(X, Y, func_type, n, font_size=12): """ Plots different fit functions for a given data fit type (linear, poly, etc) """ X, Y, n, func_type = functionator(X, Y, func_type, n)[0:4] exes = fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n)[0] LS2 = fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n)[1] cheb = fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n)[2] absdev = fitter_happier_better(X, Y, n)[3] # Labels so I don't have to type them thrice xlab = "Hours passed since Reactor Shutdown" ylab = "Reactor temperature in $^{\\circ} C$" fig1 = plt.figure(figsize=(7, 20), layout="tight") # fig1.suptitle(f"Below are the optimizations for a {func_type} fit") plt.rcParams["font.size"] = font_size # plt.rcParams["axes.labelpad"] = 10 dist = 40 pointsize = 40 point_color = "black" ax1 = plt.subplot(3, 1, 1) ax1.scatter(X, Y, s=pointsize, c=point_color) ax1.plot(exes, LS2, color="r") ax1.set_xlabel(xlab) ax1.set_ylabel(ylab, rotation=55, labelpad=(dist), fontsize=12) ax1.set_title("Least-Squares Fit") ax2 = plt.subplot(3, 1, 2) ax2.scatter(X, Y, s=pointsize, c=point_color) ax2.plot(exes, cheb, color="y") ax2.set_xlabel(xlab) ax2.set_ylabel(ylab, rotation=55, labelpad=(dist), fontsize=12) ax2.set_title("Chebyshev Fit") ax3 = plt.subplot(3, 1, 3) plt.scatter(X, Y, s=pointsize, c=point_color) plt.plot(exes, absdev, color="b") ax3.set_xlabel(xlab) ax3.set_ylabel(ylab, rotation=55, labelpad=(dist), fontsize=12) ax3.set_title("Absolute Deviation Fit") def temp_finder(X, Y, n): """ Takes X, Y, n then obtains user input for time since reactor shutdown (Will add this feature later). Returns projected temp as calculated from Chebyshev-optimized exponential fit """ """ x = float(input("How many hours has it been since the reactor shut down")) """ x = 24 X, Y, n = functionator(X, Y, "exponential", n)[0:3] # LS2_coeffs = LS2_fit(X, Y, n) cheb_coeffs = chebyshevify(X, Y, n) # abs_dev_coeffs = absdev_fit(X, Y, n) # LS2 = np.polyval(LS2_coeffs, x) cheb = np.polyval(cheb_coeffs, x) # absdev = np.polyval(abs_dev_coeffs, x) T = f"After {x} hours, we project an estimated reactor temperature of {cheb:.3f} degrees centigrade." return T X = [1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 18] Y = [580, 510, 430, 340, 290, 230] if __name__ == "__main__": main(X, Y) coeff_table(X, Y, "linear", 1) # temp_finder(X, Y) # plotter(X, Y, "polynomial") # %% ## file: requirements.txt matplotlib numpy scipy pandas tabulate ``` As can be seen in the table above, naïve observation looks to be correct, here. The exponential cooling model shows a much lower error across the dataset than do any of the polynomial models. Our model requires a focus on safety, so we'll use a Chebyshev optimization of the exponential fit. See the following section for a more thorough explanation of these choices. Since the exponential model shows the lowest error, we'll use it for extrapolation. We make a prediction for the bulk temperature of the reactor components after $24$ hours of cooling. ::: {#aba7c2a0 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} temp_finder(X, Y, 1) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ``` 'After 24 hours, we project an estimated reactor temperature of 5.084 degrees centigrade.' ``` ::: ::: # 7. Validation We chose a Chebyshev error-optimized exponential fit to model the cooling rate of this nuclear plant reactor. This is because the worst case scenario (the largest single residual) is minimized through Chebyshev optimization. We need to account not just for a comprehensive overview of error, but for the Murphy's Law example where the air is hot and humid, the cooling pool circulator is broken, and all other systems operate at minimal effectiveness. We must work under these assumptions because an overly optimistic prediction could have unacceptable consequences to the plant and its environs. We chose the exponential fit because it minimized the Chebyshev error most effectively and did so with the least over-fitting of any model. This model for the cooling rate is backed up empirically. It's a barely-modified form of Newton's Law of cooling: $$ T(t) = T_R + (T_E - T_R)e^{-kt} $$ We are only concerned with the medium, that is the reactor itself, so we don't include the $T(t)$ term, instead letting the reactor temperature take its place. \begin{aligned} T_R(t) & = (T_E - T_R)e^{-kt} \\ \ln(T_R(t)) & = \ln[(T_E-T_R)e^{-kt}] \\ \ln(T_R(t)) & = \ln(T_E - T_R) & - kt \\ \ln(T_R(t)) & = c_1 & -c_0t \\ \end{aligned} Where $T_R$ is the reactor temperature and $T_E$ is the external temperature, substituted for $T_0$ due to our assumption that the external/surrounding air temperature is constant across the range of observation. # 8. Re-evaluation and Improvement This model has a major weakness in that it approaches the problem deterministically. In reality, we would benefit from introducing randomness into our analysis, since that could provide closer estimates of daily temperature, humidity, and component failure rates while retaining this model's emphasis on multi-system error. That would enable a more efficient response to uncertainty than the chosen model where all predictions are made according to an edge case. Additionally, we assumed bulk cooling, here. Further consideration of this problem as a stochastic process would involve the treatment of heat density within the reactor. This would allow for a more accurate thermodynamical analysis and enable response to uneven coooling rates; we should be equally concerned with a single region of retained heat as with a higher temperature across the bulk. This more accurate treatment would involve solving the PDE version of the heat equation $$ \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = k \nabla^2 u + \frac{Q(x, t)}{c\rho} $$ And using what data we have to narrow down the resultant Fourier series family of solutions into a range of particular solutions. For the scope of the problem as presented, a simpler solution is valid as long as it overcorrects enough to preserve safety requirements. # Bibliography 1. "4.2A Newton's Law of Cooling." Ximera, The Ohio State University, ximera.osu.edu/ode/main/lawOfCooling/lawOfCooling. Accessed 15 June 2026. 1. Dawkins, Paul. "The Heat Equation." Paul's Online Math Notes: Differential Equations, Lamar University, tutorial.math.lamar.edu/Classes/DE/TheHeatEquation.aspx. Accessed 19 June 2026.
